narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application as a story of your journey toward becoming a lawyer, piecing together the chapters from your undergrad days through law school to predict if you'd conquer the bar exam. Your story starts strong in Year 1, landing you in the 3rd decile among your peers, but by Year 3, that slipped to the bottom decile—the model's biggest red flag, whispering doubts about sustaining top performance when it counts most. Layered on top, your LSAT score of 37 didn't quite shine as brightly as the standout applicants, pulling the narrative toward tougher odds on the bar. With a middle-family income, an undergrad GPA of 2.9, and your full-time study path as a white male, these elements combined to sketch a picture of steady but not exceptional momentum, each one nudging the prediction toward a 56.7% chance of not passing. It's not that any single part of your profile doomed the tale; it's how they intertwined, like a plot where early promise fades under pressure, making the model see higher risks ahead. You've got real potential here, especially that initial Year 1 grit—think of this as a nudge to rebuild that upward arc, maybe by targeting those decile rankings next time. Law schools want stories that end in bar success, and yours just needs a stronger climax to convince them. Hang in there; retuning your prep could rewrite this ending entirely.
